### Authors

[Shakeel Ahmad](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=Ahmad+S&cauthor_id=31915817),[Xiangjin Wei](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=Wei+X&cauthor_id=31915817),[Zhonghua Sheng](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=Sheng+Z&cauthor_id=31915817),[Peisong Hu](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=Hu+P&cauthor_id=31915817),[Shaoqing Tang](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=Tang+S&cauthor_id=31915817)

 Item in Clipboard 

 Full text links Cite

Display options

 Display options 

 Format 

Abstract
--------

Several plant pathogens severely affect crop yield and quality, thereby threatening global food security. In order to cope with this challenge, genetic improvement of plant disease resistance is required for sustainable agricultural production, for which conventional breeding is unlikely to do enough. Luckily, genome editing systems that particularly clustered regularly interspaced short palindromic repeat (CRISPR)/CRISPR-associated protein 9 (CRISPR/Cas9) has revolutionized crop improvement by enabling robust and precise targeted genome modifications. It paves the way towards new methods for genetic improvement of plant disease resistance and accelerates resistance breeding. In this review, the challenges, limitations and prospects for conventional breeding and the applications of CRISPR/Cas9 system for the development of transgene-free disease-resistant crops are discussed.

**Keywords:** CRISPR/Cas9; crop improvement; disease resistance; genome editing; phyto-pathogens; plant disease.

--- SOURCE 3: Plant and Fungal Genome Editing to Enhance ... ---
URL: https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2021.700925/full

SUMMARY:
Frontiers | Plant and Fungal Genome Editing to Enhance Plant Disease Resistance Using the CRISPR/Cas9 System In addition to applications in plants, genes encoding proteins that interact between host plants and fungal and oomycete pathogens have been targeted by CRISPR/Cas9 to elucidate the underlying molecular mechanism of host-pathogen recognition and to generate screening systems for disease resistance (Fang and Tyler, 2016; Li et al., 2018). In this review, we provide an overview of studies using CRISPR/Cas9-mediated genome editing of host plants and fungal/oomycete pathogens for improving disease resistance. Citation: Paul NC, Park S-W, Liu H, Choi S, Ma J, MacCready JS, Chilvers MI and Sang H (2021) Plant and Fungal Genome Editing to Enhance Plant Disease Resistance Using the CRISPR/Cas9 System.

FULL CONTENT:
Published Time: 2021-08-10

Front. Plant Sci., 10 August 2021

Sec. Plant Pathogen Interactions

Volume 12 - 2021 | [https://doi.org/10.3389/fpls.2021.700925](https://doi.org/10.3389/fpls.2021.700925)

This article is part of the Research Topic Plant Disease Management via CRISPR systems[View all 6 articles](https://www.frontiersin.org/research-topics/22895/plant-disease-management-via-crispr-systems/magazine)

Plant and Fungal Genome Editing to Enhance Plant Disease Resistance Using the CRISPR/Cas9 System
================================================================================================

[![Image 5: Narayan Chandra Paul,&#x;&#x;](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/55553/74)Narayan Chandra Paul](https://loop.frontiersin.org/people/55553)1,2†‡[![Image 6: Sung-Won Park&#x;&#x;](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/1352569/74)Sung-Won Park](https://loop.frontiersin.org/people/1352569)1†‡![Image 7: Sungyu Choi&#x;](https://loop.frontiersin.org/cdn/images/profile/default_32.jpg)Haifeng Liu 1‡![Image 8: Sungyu Choi&#x;](https://loop.frontiersin.org/cdn/images/profile/default_32.jpg)Sungyu Choi 1‡[![Image 9: Jihyeon Ma](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/1352617/74)Jihyeon Ma](https://loop.frontiersin.org/people/1352617)1[![Image 10: Joshua S. MacCready&#x;](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/1322660/74)Joshua S. MacCready](https://loop.frontiersin.org/people/1322660)3‡[![Image 11: Martin I. Chilvers&#x;](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/556367/74)Martin I. Chilvers](https://loop.frontiersin.org/people/556367)3‡[![Image 12: Hyunkyu Sang,*&#x;](https://loop.frontiersin.org/images/profile/1315511/74)Hyunkyu Sang](https://loop.frontiersin.org/people/1315511)1,2*‡

*   1 Department of Integrative Food, Bioscience and Biotechnology, Chonnam National University, Gwangju, South Korea
*   2 Kumho Life Science Laboratory, Chonnam National University, Gwangju, South Korea
*   3 Department of Plant, Soil and Microbial Sciences, Michigan State University, East Lansing, MI, United States

Crop production has been substantially reduced by devastating fungal and oomycete pathogens, and these pathogens continue to threaten global food security. Although chemical and cultural controls have been used for crop protection, these involve continuous costs and time and fungicide resistance among plant pathogens has been increasingly reported. The most efficient way to protect crops from plant pathogens is cultivation of disease-resistant cultivars. However, traditional breeding approaches are laborious and time intensive. Recently, the CRISPR/Cas9 system has been utilized to enhance disease resistance among different crops such as rice, cacao, wheat, tomato, and grape. This system allows for precise genome editing of various organisms via RNA-guided DNA endonuclease activity. Beyond genome editing in crops, editing the genomes of fungal and oomycete pathogens can also provide new strategies for plant disease management. This review focuses on the recent studies of plant disease resistance against fungal and oomycete pathogens using the CRISPR/Cas9 system. For long-term plant disease management, the targeting of multiple plant disease resistance mechanisms with CRISPR/Cas9 and insights gained by probing fungal and oomycete genomes with this system will be powerful approaches.

Introduction
------------

An increasing human population needs to have sufficient food supplies. With a projected global population in 2050 of 9.2 billion, this creates a significant increase in demand for food. Not only is global food security challenged by an increasing population and climate change, but by a multitude of evolving, emerging, and introduced plant pathogens ([Anderson et al., 2004](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2021.700925/full#B3); [Bebber and Gurr, 2015](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2021.700925/full#B5)). The most destructive plant pathogens are the fungi and oomycetes, which are taxonomically distinct but have similar filamentous growth and host infection structures ([Dong et al., 2015](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2021.700925/full#B16)). These pathogens can destroy crops in a short period of time and cause severe famine such as the Irish potato famine caused by the oomycete pathogen _Phytophthora infestans_ ([Turner, 2005](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2021.700925/full#B70); [Fones et al., 2020](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2021.700925/full#B21)) and the Bengal famine caused by the rice brown spot fungal pathogen, _Cochliobolus miyabeanus_ ([Chakrabarti, 2001](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2021.700925/full#B9); [Corredor-Moreno and Saunders, 2020](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2021.700925/full#B11)). The use of pesticides, cultural, and cultivation practices can provide a level of protection from plant disease. However, pesticides can cause severe environmental devastation and are rapidly losing efficacy due to pathogen evolution, and they are often strictly regulated to minimize unwanted side effects ([McDonald and Stukenbrock, 2016](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2021.700925/full#B49); [Vannier et al., 2019](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2021.700925/full#B73); [Esse et al., 2020](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2021.700925/full#B17); [Tyagi et al., 2021](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2021.700925/full#B71)). The development of plant varieties with inherent disease resistance through breeding provides an environmentally friendly and often complimentary approach to plant disease management ([Sánchez-Martín and Keller, 2019](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2021.700925/full#B61)). Plant breeding for disease-resistance generally relies on identifying plants that carry considerable disease resistance traits, the growth, and development of breeding candidates in a disease-conducive setting, and finally, selecting disease resistance individuals that retain yield.

Current plant breeding methods to obtain productive, disease free, nutritious, and safe crops utilize both conventional and molecular methods ([Yin and Qiu, 2019](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2021.700925/full#B85); [Kaiser et al., 2020](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2021.700925/full#B29); [Tyagi et al., 2021](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2021.700925/full#B71)). These approaches have been developed, modified, and re-examined over time. Conventional breeding strategies include pure line selection, pedigree, interspecific hybrids, and back-cross methods. Alternatively, molecular approaches contain variation within multiple genes, marker-assisted breeding, transgenic and tissue culture methods, gene silencing, and plant susceptibility alleles ([Capriotti et al., 2020](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2021.700925/full#B7)). Conventional breeding, mutation breeding, and transgenic technology have been successful over the last few decades, but several limitations exist, including: (i) intensive labor cost, (ii) inherent genetic variation within plant populations, and (iii) transfer of undesirable genes or traits along with desired resistance genes ([Gao, 2018](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2021.700925/full#B23); [Ahmad et al., 2020](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2021.700925/full#B2)).

In recent years, new breeding technologies such as meganucleases (MNs), zinc-finger nucleases (ZFNs), transcription-activator-like (TAL) effector nucleases (TALENs), and clustered regularly interspaced palindromic repeats (CRISPR) and CRISPR-associated protein 9 (Cas9) endonucleases have been developed to overcome these limitations ([Townsend et al., 2009](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2021.700925/full#B69); [Gao et al., 2010](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2021.700925/full#B24); [Cermak et al., 2011](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2021.700925/full#B8); [Li et al., 2012](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2021.700925/full#B41), [2013](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2021.700925/full#B37); [Nekrasov et al., 2013](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2021.700925/full#B54); [Shan et al., 2013](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2021.700925/full#B66)). These new breeding technologies allow precise genetic modifications of single or multiple gene targets in plants ([Borrelli et al., 2018](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2021.700925/full#B6)). MNs, ZFNs, TALENs and CRISPR/Cas9 are sequence-specific nucleases that cleave target DNA. Double-stranded breaks present in DNA are repaired by host cell repair mechanisms such as homology-directed repair (HDR) or non-homologous end-joining (NHEJ), but small indels (insertions/deletions) can occur within the target region ([Langner et al., 2018](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/plant-science/articles/10.3389/fpls.2021.700925/full#B34)). Although MNs, ZFNs, and TALENs were applied before the introduction of CRISPR/Cas9, the three techniques have not been widely utilized for plant breeding due to the need for complex protein engineering systems. enriched_points.expanded_content: Advancements such as CRISPR/Cas9 have revolutionized genetic engineering, enabling precise modifications that enhance plant disease resistance and offer sustainable agricultural solutions.
CRISPR/Cas9 facilitates targeted genome editing, allowing for the introduction of resistance genes effectively, as demonstrated in crops like rice and tomato, enhancing yield and resilience.

enriched_points.point_title: Importance of genomic studies for resistance
enriched_points.expanded_content: Genomic studies provide insights into plant-pathogen interactions, guiding the identification of resistance genes and facilitating rapid breeding of disease-resistant varieties.
